content:
# John Chapter 20 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 20 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 20:1 — "on the first day of the week" vs. "Early on Sunday morning" and "while it was still dark"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "on the first [day] of the week, Maria the Magdalene comes early, [while] darkness [was] still [upon the land]" | Literal; historical present "comes"; supplies "[upon the land]" |
| LOGOS | "on the first day of the week Mary Magdalene came to the tomb early, while it was still dark" | Past tense; "while it was still dark" |
| PNEUMA | "Early on Sunday morning, while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene came to the tomb and found that the stone had been rolled away from the entrance" | "Sunday morning" for "first day of the week"; "rolled away" adds detail; "entrance" not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The first day of the week" preserves the Jewish calendrical reference. PNEUMA's "Sunday morning" is anachronistic (the word "Sunday" is not in the text). Also, the Greek says the stone was "taken away" (ermenon), not "rolled away."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:2 — "They have taken the Lord out of the tomb" vs. "They have taken the Lord's body out of the tomb"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "They took the Lord from the tomb" | Literal Eran ton kurion ek tou mnEmeiou |
| LOGOS | "They have taken the Lord out of the tomb" | Same with present perfect |
| PNEUMA | "They have taken the Lord's body out of the tomb" | Adds "body" — not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Mary says "the Lord" (ton kurion), not "the Lord's body" — her language already shows reverence and a refusal to reduce Jesus to a corpse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:7 — "the face cloth" vs. "the cloth that was upon the head of him" vs. "the cloth that had covered Jesus' head"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the cloth that was upon the head of him, not lying with the linen cloths but apart, having been rolled up into one place" | Literal soudarion; "rolled up" for entetuligmenon |
| LOGOS | "the face cloth, which had been on Jesus' head, not lying with the linen cloths but folded up in a place by itself" | "Face cloth" for soudarion; "folded up" for entetuligmenon |
| PNEUMA | "the cloth that had covered Jesus' head was folded up and lying apart from the other wrappings" | "Covered" for epi tEs kephalEs; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Face cloth" is a good translation of soudarion (a cloth for wiping sweat, also used in burial). "Folded up" captures entetuligmenon (wrapped/rolled together). All three capture the key detail — the burial cloths were undisturbed, not ripped off.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor differences; the essential detail is consistent.

---

## 20:9 — "they did not understand the Scripture" vs. "they did not know the scripture" vs. "they still hadn't understood the Scriptures"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not yet did they know the scripture" | Literal Edeisan — "know" for oida |
| LOGOS | "they did not understand the Scripture" | "Understand" — interpretive rendering of oida in context |
| PNEUMA | "they still hadn't understood the Scriptures that said Jesus must rise from the dead" | Adds "that said" — interpretive expansion; pluralizes "Scriptures" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Understand" captures the sense of oida here (knowing/comprehending the significance of Scripture). PNEUMA adds "that said" which shifts from not understanding to not having read — a different issue.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:12 — "two angels in white" vs. "two white-robed angels"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "two angels in white" | Literal duo angelous en leukois |
| LOGOS | "two angels in white" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "two white-robed angels" | "White-robed" — adjectival compound |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "two angels in white." "In white" preserves the Greek prepositional phrase (en leukois) which describes their clothing without specifying "robed."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor stylistic difference.

---

## 20:13 — "Woman, why are you weeping?" vs. "Dear woman, why are you crying?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Woman, why do you weep?" | Literal gunai, ti klaieis |
| LOGOS | "Woman, why are you weeping?" | Same; progressive aspect |
| PNEUMA | "Dear woman, why are you crying?" | Adds "Dear"; "crying" for klaiO |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. As in 19:26, adding "Dear" softens the formal Greek address. "Weeping" is the traditional rendering of klaiO in this passage and carries more emotional weight than "crying."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:15 — "Whom are you seeking?" vs. "Who are you looking for?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Whom do you seek?" | Literal tina zEteis — echoes 18:4, 7 |
| LOGOS | "Whom are you seeking?" | Same; progressive |
| PNEUMA | "Who are you looking for?" | Colloquial; "looking for" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Whom are you seeking?" This question echoes the arrest scene (18:4, 7) — the same verb zEteO — creating a deliberate structural parallel between the garden of arrest and the garden of resurrection. The formal register preserves this.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:16 — "in Aramaic" vs. "in Hebrew"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in Hebrew, 'Rabbouni!'" | Literal HebraistE |
| LOGOS | "in Aramaic, 'Rabboni!'" | Interprets HebraistE as Aramaic |
| PNEUMA | "'Rabboni!' (which is Hebrew for 'Teacher')" | "Hebrew" with parenthetical gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in Aramaic." Consistent with the 19:13 decision. Rabboni is an Aramaic word.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:17 — "Do not cling to me" vs. "Do not touch me" vs. "Don't cling to me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not touch me" | Literal mE mou haptou — present imperative "stop touching/holding" |
| LOGOS | "Do not cling to me" | Interprets haptou as clinging (present imperative = ongoing action) |
| PNEUMA | "Don't cling to me" | Same as LOGOS; colloquial contraction |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Do not cling to me." The present imperative mE + haptou means "stop holding on to me" — not a prohibition of touch but of continued clinging. "Cling" captures this correctly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "touch" is misleading — it suggests Jesus forbids any physical contact, which contradicts v.27 where he invites Thomas to touch him.

---

## 20:17 — "I am ascending to my Father and your Father" vs. "I ascend to the Father of me and Father of you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I ascend to the Father of me and Father of you, and God of me and God of you" | Literal; present tense "I ascend"; "Father of me" |
| LOGOS | "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God" | Progressive present; natural possessives |
| PNEUMA | "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three agree on the substance. LOGOS's "I am ascending" captures the present tense (anabainO) as an imminent/in-progress action.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Note: Jesus says "my Father and your Father" — not "our Father" — preserving the distinction between his unique sonship and the disciples' derived sonship.

---

## 20:19 — "Jesus came and stood among them" vs. "Suddenly, Jesus was standing there among them!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Yeshua came and stood in the middle and says to them" | Literal; historical present |
| LOGOS | "Jesus came and stood among them and said to them" | Past tense; "among" |
| PNEUMA | "Suddenly, Jesus was standing there among them! 'Peace be with you,' he said." | Adds "Suddenly"; exclamation point adds drama |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek does not say "suddenly" — Elthen ho IEsous kai estE eis to meson ("Jesus came and stood in the middle"). The arrival through locked doors is remarkable enough without adding drama.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:23 — "if you withhold forgiveness from any, it is withheld" vs. "if you retain those of any, they have been retained" vs. "If you do not forgive them, they are not forgiven"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if you retain [those] of any, they have been retained" | Literal kratEte — "retain/hold fast"; perfect tense "have been retained" |
| LOGOS | "if you withhold forgiveness from any, it is withheld" | "Withhold" for kratEte; present tense in English |
| PNEUMA | "If you do not forgive them, they are not forgiven" | Simplifies; drops the "retain/withhold" verb entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Withhold forgiveness" captures kratEte (hold fast/retain) in context. PNEUMA's simplification loses the active verb — the disciples are given authority to actively retain sins, not merely to fail to forgive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:25 — "the mark of the nails" vs. "the nail wounds" and "I will never believe" vs. "I won't believe it"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unless I see in the hands of him the mark of the nails... I will by no means believe" | Literal ton tupon tOn hElOn; ou mE pisteusO (emphatic negation) |
| LOGOS | "unless I see in his hands the mark of the nails... I will never believe" | "Mark of the nails" preserved; "never" for ou mE |
| PNEUMA | "I won't believe it unless I see the nail wounds in his hands, put my fingers into them, and place my hand into the wound in his side" | "Nail wounds" for tupon tOn hElOn; "wound in his side" adds "wound" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The mark of the nails" (ton tupon tOn hElOn) is more precise than "nail wounds" — tupos means "mark/imprint/pattern," not wound. "I will never believe" preserves the emphatic ou mE double negative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:27 — "Do not disbelieve, but believe" vs. "do not become unbelieving but believing" vs. "Don't be faithless any longer. Believe!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not become unbelieving but believing" | Literal mE ginou apistos alla pistos |
| LOGOS | "Do not disbelieve, but believe" | Verb forms for adjectives; concise |
| PNEUMA | "Don't be faithless any longer. Believe!" | "Any longer" adds time element; exclamation; two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek is mE ginou apistos alla pistos — "do not become unbelieving but believing." LOGOS's "Do not disbelieve, but believe" is rhythmically clean and captures the contrast. PNEUMA's "any longer" implies Thomas has been faithless for an extended period, which adds a judgment not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:29 — "Have you believed because you have seen me?" vs. "You believe because you have seen me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Because you have seen me, you have believed" | Statement (indicative) |
| LOGOS | "Have you believed because you have seen me?" | Question (with question mark) |
| PNEUMA | "You believe because you have seen me" | Statement (indicative, present tense) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's question form. The Greek hoti heOrakas me pepisteukas can be read as either statement or question. The question reading is more pastorally nuanced — Jesus is not criticizing Thomas but gently probing, then pronouncing a beatitude on future believers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The question form allows for more interpretive openness.

---

## 20:30 — "signs" vs. "miraculous signs"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "many other signs therefore also the Yeshua did" | Literal sEmeia |
| LOGOS | "Jesus did many other signs" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "The disciples saw Jesus do many other miraculous signs" | Adds "miraculous" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "signs." SEmeion in John means "sign" — a pointer to deeper meaning. Adding "miraculous" reduces the sign to its wonder-aspect and obscures the Johannine concept.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 20:31 — "the Christ" vs. "the Messiah" and "life in his name" vs. "life by the power of his name"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Yeshua is the Christos, the Son of the God, and that believing you may have life in the name of him" | Transliterates Christos; "in the name" |
| LOGOS | "Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name" | "The Christ"; "in his name" |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, and that by believing in him you will have life by the power of his name" | "Messiah" for Christos; "by the power of" adds "power"; "will have" — future indicative |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The Christ" is the standard NT rendering of ho Christos in this confessional context. PNEUMA's "by the power of" adds a word (dynamis/power) not in the text. "May have" (subjunctive) is more faithful than "will have" (indicative).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. This is the purpose statement of the entire Gospel — every word must be precise.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adding words not in Greek | 7 | "Sunday" (v.1), "body" (v.2), "Dear" (v.13), "Suddenly" (v.19), "miraculous" (v.30), "power" (v.31), "any longer" (v.27) |
| PNEUMA changing grammatical mood | 2 | Statement to exclamation (vv.27, 19), subjunctive to indicative (v.31) |
| Key Johannine term preservation | 3 | sEmeion: signs (v.30), tupos: mark (v.25), kratEte: withhold/retain (v.23) |
| HebraistE rendering | 1 | v.16 — Aramaic/Hebrew (consistent with prior decisions) |
| Ego eimi / "I am" resonance | 0 | No ego eimi statements in this chapter |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA near-identical | ~28 | Most verses identical |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

John 20 is the resurrection climax, and the translation divergences are concentrated in the most emotionally and theologically charged moments. The most important decision is v.17 — "Do not cling to me" (LOGOS) vs. "Do not touch me" (EMET). EMET's rendering has caused centuries of confusion (the Noli me tangere tradition), while LOGOS correctly identifies the present imperative as "stop clinging" rather than "don't touch."

The rendering of v.29 (question vs. statement) is a significant interpretive choice: LOGOS's question form ("Have you believed because you have seen me?") is gentler and more theologically open than EMET's or PNEUMA's flat statements. It allows the beatitude that follows ("Blessed are those who have not seen...") to function as a complement rather than a contrast.

The chapter's concluding purpose statement (v.31) is the most consequential verse for translation: "these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God." Every word in this summary has been a contested rendering throughout the Gospel: "believe" (pisteuO), "Christ" (Christos), "Son of God" (huios tou theou), "life" (zOE), "name" (onoma). SOPHIA's consensus here validates every prior decision.

---
*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — 2026-03-05*
